articles.title: Higher education is less associated with economic parity for women in highly hierarchical cultures
articles.summary: Higher education is often viewed as a great equalizer. However, new research suggests the association between a college degree and women's economic equality is significantly weaker in countries that favor rigid social hierarchies and traditional gender roles.
articles.aiSummary: Higher education is often viewed as a great equalizer. However, new research suggests the association between a college degree and women's economic equality is significantly weaker in countries that favor rigid social hierarchies and traditional gender roles.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Higher education is often viewed as a great equalizer. However, new research suggests the association between a college degree and women's economic equality is significantly weaker in countries that favor rigid social hierarchies and traditional gender roles.

articles.title: Texting a stranger works better to fight loneliness than talking to an AI chatbot
articles.summary: Can an artificial intelligence bot cure feelings of isolation? A new psychology experiment finds that while AI companions can offer emotional support, only daily texting with a real human peer actually reduces loneliness over time.
articles.aiSummary: Can an artificial intelligence bot cure feelings of isolation? A new psychology experiment finds that while AI companions can offer emotional support, only daily texting with a real human peer actually reduces loneliness over time.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Can an artificial intelligence bot cure feelings of isolation? A new psychology experiment finds that while AI companions can offer emotional support, only daily texting with a real human peer actually reduces loneliness over time.

articles.title: What It’s Like to Be…an Air Traffic Controller
articles.summary: Sequencing departures like a moving puzzle, scrambling when a jet takes off without clearance, and catching what radar misses with a glance out the window with Michael Rejent, an air traffic controller. articles.title: What It’s Like to Be…a Flight Attendant
articles.summary: Hugging a mother through an anxiety attack, bidding for the best routes, and absorbing strangers' confessions at 35,000 feet with Linda Beall, a flight attendant. articles.title: More than 50 percent of adults worry about their libido, new study finds
articles.summary: Everyday stressors, mental health challenges, and relationship dynamics play a major role in how people perceive their sexual desire. New research reveals that these factors lead more than half of adults to worry about their libido.
articles.aiSummary: Everyday stressors, mental health challenges, and relationship dynamics play a major role in how people perceive their sexual desire. New research reveals that these factors lead more than half of adults to worry about their libido.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Everyday stressors, mental health challenges, and relationship dynamics play a major role in how people perceive their sexual desire. New research reveals that these factors lead more than half of adults to worry about their libido.

articles.title: Scientists may have finally found how Alzheimer's spreads through the brain
articles.summary: A common brain protein may be giving Alzheimer’s disease an unexpected way to spread, carrying toxic Tau proteins from damaged neurons into healthy ones. By blocking these harmful protein packages before they reach new cells, researchers believe it may one day be possible to slow the disease's relentless progression.
articles.aiSummary: A common brain protein may be giving Alzheimer’s disease an unexpected way to spread, carrying toxic Tau proteins from damaged neurons into healthy ones. By blocking these harmful protein packages before they reach new cells, researchers believe it may one day be possible to slow the disease's relentless progression.

By blocking these harmful protein packages before they reach new cells, researchers believe it may one day be possible to slow the disease's relentless progression.

articles.title: Scientists say creatine may help fight depression
articles.summary: Creatine is best known as a muscle-building supplement, but scientists are now investigating whether it could also help treat depression by boosting the brain's energy supply. A new review examined five randomized clinical trials involving 238 participants and found mixed results. Two studies, both involving women with major depressive disorder, reported that adding creatine to standard treatment improved symptoms, while three others found no meaningful benefit.

articles.title: When do humans reach their psychological peak? A new study points to late midlife
articles.summary: While raw processing speed declines after your 20s, a combination of emotional intelligence, accumulated knowledge, and mature personality traits pushes optimal human functioning into late midlife. New research reveals the ideal age for complex decision-making.
articles.aiSummary: While raw processing speed declines after your 20s, a combination of emotional intelligence, accumulated knowledge, and mature personality traits pushes optimal human functioning into late midlife. New research reveals the ideal age for complex decision-making.
articles.aiSummaryEn: While raw processing speed declines after your 20s, a combination of emotional intelligence, accumulated knowledge, and mature personality traits pushes optimal human functioning into late midlife. New research reveals the ideal age for complex decision-making.

articles.title: Democrats are disproportionately driving the rise in angry congressional tweets, study finds
articles.summary: A recent study of over two million tweets suggests members of Congress are increasingly using angry rhetoric on social media. Researchers found that Democratic lawmakers are disproportionately driving this trend of digital outrage to build their political brands.
articles.aiSummary: A recent study of over two million tweets suggests members of Congress are increasingly using angry rhetoric on social media. Researchers found that Democratic lawmakers are disproportionately driving this trend of digital outrage to build their political brands.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A recent study of over two million tweets suggests members of Congress are increasingly using angry rhetoric on social media. Researchers found that Democratic lawmakers are disproportionately driving this trend of digital outrage to build their political brands.

articles.title: Love addiction, emotional dependence, and manic love have distinct psychological profiles
articles.summary: When romantic relationships become obsessive or overly dependent, the underlying psychology can vary significantly. A new review reveals that manic love, emotional dependence, and love addiction each have distinct psychological profiles and should be treated as separate issues.
articles.aiSummary: When romantic relationships become obsessive or overly dependent, the underlying psychology can vary significantly. A new review reveals that manic love, emotional dependence, and love addiction each have distinct psychological profiles and should be treated as separate issues.
articles.aiSummaryEn: When romantic relationships become obsessive or overly dependent, the underlying psychology can vary significantly. A new review reveals that manic love, emotional dependence, and love addiction each have distinct psychological profiles and should be treated as separate issues.

articles.title: Classic psychedelics linked to lasting increases in openness and drops in neuroticism
articles.summary: A new review of nearly 50 studies suggests classic psychedelics like psilocybin and LSD can cause lasting personality changes. Research links the use of these substances to long-term increases in openness and significant drops in neuroticism.
articles.aiSummary: A new review of nearly 50 studies suggests classic psychedelics like psilocybin and LSD can cause lasting personality changes. Research links the use of these substances to long-term increases in openness and significant drops in neuroticism.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new review of nearly 50 studies suggests classic psychedelics like psilocybin and LSD can cause lasting personality changes. Research links the use of these substances to long-term increases in openness and significant drops in neuroticism.

articles.title: Scientists discover why some brains resist Alzheimer's
articles.summary: Some brains appear to fight back against Alzheimer's by helping immature brain cells survive damage instead of succumbing to it. Understanding this natural resilience could point researchers toward entirely new ways to protect memory and slow dementia.
articles.aiSummary: Some brains appear to fight back against Alzheimer's by helping immature brain cells survive damage instead of succumbing to it. Understanding this natural resilience could point researchers toward entirely new ways to protect memory and slow dementia.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Some brains appear to fight back against Alzheimer's by helping immature brain cells survive damage instead of succumbing to it. Understanding this natural resilience could point researchers toward entirely new ways to protect memory and slow dementia.

articles.title: Drinking to cope with stress may permanently rewire your brain
articles.summary: Using alcohol to cope with stress when young may permanently alter the brain, making it harder to adapt to challenges and increasing the risk of returning to drinking later in life. Researchers also found signs of brain damage associated with early dementia, suggesting the effects can linger long after alcohol use has stopped.
articles.aiSummary: Using alcohol to cope with stress when young may permanently alter the brain, making it harder to adapt to challenges and increasing the risk of returning to drinking later in life. Researchers also found signs of brain damage associated with early dementia, suggesting the effects can linger long after alcohol use has stopped.

Researchers also found signs of brain damage associated with early dementia, suggesting the effects can linger long after alcohol use has stopped.

articles.title: Declining state church membership tied to dropping birth rates in Finland
articles.summary: A massive analysis of population data in Finland links the decline in national church membership with the country's dropping birth rate. As secularization rises, couples that share a religious affiliation are becoming rarer, further suppressing first-time parenthood.
articles.aiSummary: A massive analysis of population data in Finland links the decline in national church membership with the country's dropping birth rate. As secularization rises, couples that share a religious affiliation are becoming rarer, further suppressing first-time parenthood.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A massive analysis of population data in Finland links the decline in national church membership with the country's dropping birth rate. As secularization rises, couples that share a religious affiliation are becoming rarer, further suppressing first-time parenthood.

articles.title: How Can We Feel Loved If We Don’t Feel Known?
articles.summary: For Sonja, researching happiness means being asked over and over again, “What is the secret to happiness?” Sonja has always dreaded that question. It’s ridiculous, it’s reductive, and it’s restrictive. But mostly she has dreaded it because she has never had a solid, science-based answer that she could offer with any confidence. There is no secret to happiness. But there are huge contributors to happiness, and some of them are more powerful than others. Sonja resists being pinned down to just one. But if she were pressed to give an answer, she now has one. She found it when she met Harry, who s

articles.title: Voters find AI-generated debate answers more authentic than real political speech
articles.summary: A recent study suggests that voters easily mistake AI-generated political content for reality. Participants actually rated fake debate responses as more genuine and on-topic than the exact answers provided by the real politicians.
articles.aiSummary: A recent study suggests that voters easily mistake AI-generated political content for reality. Participants actually rated fake debate responses as more genuine and on-topic than the exact answers provided by the real politicians.

articles.title: What It’s Like to Be…an Airline Pilot
articles.summary: Shooting cat-three landings in the fog, using the “voice of God” on unruly passengers, and declaring Mayday after an engine fire with Paul Drusch, a commercial airline pilot. Why does the pilot’s paycheck start with the parking brake? And what does “sterile cockpit” mean? (Spoiler: it doesn’t mean “clean.”) [buzzsprout episode='19294742' player='true'] View the episode transcript About What It's Like to Be... articles.title: What It’s Like to Be…a Clinical Ethicist
articles.summary: Untangling who gets to speak for a dying patient, weighing a treatment's benefits against its burdens, and searching for clarity in the grayest corners of healthcare with Esther Berkowitz, a clinical ethicist. articles.title: Brain signal chaos increases during an active migraine attack
articles.summary: People with frequent migraines lose natural unpredictability in their brain signals over time. A recent brain imaging study reveals that sudden migraine attacks might serve as a chaotic reset switch, temporarily restoring flexibility to rigid neural networks.
articles.aiSummary: People with frequent migraines lose natural unpredictability in their brain signals over time. A recent brain imaging study reveals that sudden migraine attacks might serve as a chaotic reset switch, temporarily restoring flexibility to rigid neural networks.

articles.title: Announcing Our Summer Book Club and Podcast
articles.summary: Each summer, we read a novel and explore its themes through conversations with leading thinkers at the intersection of science and culture. This summer, we’ll read Flights by Polish author Olga Tokarczuk, the recipient of the 2018 Nobel Prize in literature. Flights is an enigma of a novel—part memoir, part short story collection, part ethnography of travel—that explores the human impulse toward movement; the perpetual tension between exploring somewhere new or staying put. “Clearly I did not inherit whatever gene it is that makes it so when you linger in a place you start to put down roots,” T
